dialogue.utterance: Code 3 on 911 for medical emergency, apartment A in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Taylor, an EMT. What happened here tonight?
She was fine and just passed out. I could not wake her up.
How long ago did she collapse?
About 10 minutes ago, I think. We were sitting on the porch drinking beers earlier tonight.
Ma'am, can you hear me? Feel this squeeze of your hand for pain response.
No eye opening in response to pain. GCS is 3 overall - E1, V1, M1.
Airway is compromised - snoring respirations. Performing jaw thrust maneuver immediately.
Nasopharyngeal airway placed in right nostril, using 26Fr size. Airway is now secured with NPA.
Respiratory rate is 4-6 per minute, snoring, and shallow. Ripping into the O2 bottle now, let's go to BVM support.
Respiratory rate still 4-6 per minute with assisted BVM breaths at 10-12 per minute. We are manually breathing for her due to low drive and irregular pattern.
SpO2 is down to 0 percent initially. After we attached BVM with 25LPM oxygen, it rose to around 50-60, and now it is stabilizing at 56 percent. Let's keep that updated every few minutes.
Strong pulse found with carotid and radial, rate approximately 80 beats per minute. Skin is warm and dry, no cyanosis observed as of now.
Initial blood pressure is 0/0 mmHg - probably due to ineffective breathing or cardiac standstill. Let me recheck after breaths have stabilized a bit further.

BVM mask on now with 25LPM oxygen. Positioning her head at a slight tilt for better airway patency. Watch for capnography setup next - this is a critical step in monitoring her CO2 status. Capnography tube going on now, inline style to monitor EtCO2 levels while collecting breath samples. With inline capnography we'll be tracking effectiveness of breaths more accurately from here out. It's essential for confirming correct airway placement and tracking comeback or complications. Plug it in.

BVM is up and running with 25LPM oxygen. Capnography is showing up on the device, looks like we have End-tidal CO2 coming through. This will help us see whether her breathing improves with interventions.

Pinpoint pupils are present. Combined with respiratory failure and unresponsiveness - indicating opioid overdose with high degree of confidence. Naloxone reversal would be highly appropriate here. Check IV access next and let's prepare Narcan for IV push if needed. IV line access is key here, though IV was attempted in the left arm initially and failed. We'll try left wrist now as per protocol. Let's secure IV and prep Narcan now for administration in case she continues to deteriorate or not respond sufficiently to current BVM support and oxygen.

Beginning 4-lead ECG monitoring. It is showing sinus rhythm at the moment. Initial rhythm looks stable, no arrhythmias detected so far. Keep monitoring, and update once we have more data on SpO2 returns fully and as she potentially comes back from unresponsiveness post Narcan if needed down the line. Sinus now, we'll reassess trend and repeat monitoring in a minute or so if clinically useful for any interventions moving ahead to ensure no cardiac complications. I accept this rhythm as baseline at first reading right now. ECG pads are double-checked. No artifacts spotted yet on visual inspection. Stabilizing remaining electrode pads on the torso to ensure it continues working overall. Let me plug in the lead cable now. It's clear of first use and I can see the data on the display now. That confirms baseline sinus rhythm. Final step - monitor for any changes as we go through protocols and her condition stabilizes or changes post intervention. If things remain consistent, that's good to know, especially for emergency transport or any observation in ED upon arrival post-stabilization per instructions for follow-up care leadership. We'll track from here before proceeding with the Narcan, ensuring all access is secure before escalating drug use to wake her up if necessary. No ETCO2 spikes or rhythm changes yet from the ECG, so that's manageable in this phase. We're on track for protocol 7-1 which is targeted at opioid overdose specifically fitting this medical case. I'll snapshot this moment as per monitoring sequence to close on this step before moving to drug administration proper. The ECG still indeed shows sinus rhythm with no signs of ischaemia or other disturbances. Allowed to proceed with next steps. Thank you for your confirmation and support on this set up. I'll now plug this into larger clinical picture for treatment guidance after next sections are setup. Let's proceed now with interventions including IV Narcan IV push once IV line is secured in L wrist mueh. We need to re-establish baseline after that push is made. I'll update now after IV is secured since Narcan needs IV push. So keep an eye on the main line setup before that step. Prepaing Narcan 0.25mg now per standing order for this emergency situation. Double-checking cross-tag with partner to confirm. Safe doses, minimal numbers involved here. Thank you for your team's support. Good to go for establishing lifeline here with Narcan spike. Preparation complete. I'll update once administered and response noted down. Then assess if additional push or access necessary before other steps are taken for stabilization in full. This step takes precedence while she's in deregulated condition, then we can focus on monitoring re-assessment in bulk for SpO2 levels and EKG in tandem. Let's move forward as best as we can before interventions are cascaded, working on IV line now per setups.

Glucose is 181 mg/dL, baseline for this patient-no hypoglycemia at this time. Good to know for any additional treatment considerations once she reawakens from a critical state. Proceed with protocol while this notate remains clear. No urgent action required for this initial blood sugar reading, therefore we proceed. BSG monitoring not required currently since the patient is now responsive post-Naloxone administration. Additional glucose checks temporarily not relevant for her condition unless complications arise down the pipeline. That said, we have one positive for all indicators here at this moment. Let proceed with next protocol for now per standing orders. I'll take note of this and carry it over for documentation per usual.

Narcan on board. Double-check the dosage with partner, please. Cross-check the vial and flush to ensure 0.25 mg is ready to administer. Let's make sure we've got a full two-provider check before proceeding with naloxone intravenously so we follow protocol correctly here. Integration of cross-check is essential here for correct dosage and procedure management as per routine. Safety first with Narcan delivery, since overdoses can be severe and dose misses cause backup steps and potential complications. Take your time, confirm both visually and verbally. Final doors confirmed. Let's lock in with IV push now. Let's do this carefully and safely. Alright, dose confirmed. We are ready for push. Full verification done, correct dose safe for administration pre IV push. Going in now as instructed per standing order protocol 7-1 while still maintaining current airway with NPA and BVM. Allowed to proceed per team review. Ready for cross-administration. Now IV push administered. Complete at 01:00 as per ePCR log entry on Narcan. Partner assisting on Narcan flush while this occurred and monitoring is in progress for immediate effectiveness onset. Pulse and breath shows response now on what we've done in setups. Support now up and going to note from here on. Moving forward to next step while she regains awareness alongside recovery tracks. Documenting now for this critical push while current uptake is in progress per the Narcan protocol. Next monitoring sequence scheduled for from here on forward. I'll flag this as completed section for procedure recognition while allied procedures start on next steps. She started showing some initial signs of response after Narcan now, recovery beginning to show up in specific vital readings over recent intervals again. Let's keep an eye on the changing here via pulse and SpO2, as well as in the next hand.

Defibrillation pads are attached in tandem with 4-lead monitoring for full cardiac oversight at this time. Proceeding with monitoring, description of procedure, and timing of application here as per per Code guidance and patient safety protocols. Monitoring apparatus set for real-time tracking in tandem with EKG and overall access setup once Narcan has been administered and response is checked in. That done, and full readiness established now for next phase in patient stabilization. Confirm all readings before moving ahead in full.

Were there any other symptoms reported before this incident happened? Any chest pain or altered physical condition she mentioned during the day? Concerned about some physical damage less than obvious yet riskier medical ones were involved for her current state.

No. She fell asleep suddenly, and her eyes looked like they glazed over. She looked limp like something was wrong that I did not recognize before with full awareness. That just happened out of the blue and quite fast as I could see. She was not pre-telling any trouble or negative indicators at first glance by me as a friend accompanying her in that time on the porch. No direct medical signs mentioned by her either at the time before collapse as per her own words told to me as friend.

Did she eat or drink anything before this event? Any alcohol consumption or other liquids recently down the line? Important to gather all baseline data for accurate history tracking and treatment guidance for ED down the line post-stabilization. Need full answer if possible before full transit is induced to ED. Let's make sure intake was reported properly here as per field EPCRs as we go for the record.

She had about 8 beers and that's probably it. No solid foods noted during downtime or evening before incident as per my view from sitting together on the porch. No mention of other intake pre-collapse, as before described. As far as I know, she had several cokes with burgers for dinner earlier that day. No other records of recent intake followed. That said, 8 beers is a significant volume for a woman, potentially leading to toxicity by itself if unaware of intake's effects with existing medical conditions. But per her account, she did not take any other substances when I was around her, which just helps with tracking here. I know she denied drug use that night as best as I can recall from our conversation during or prior to collapse moment. It just was 8 beers otherwise. For sure there was that much. That's what she told me while drinking that night on the porch with others as well. It would be noted for honesty. We did not partake in other drugs or take anything else, no along with her. It was a casual evening for us when this happened without further complications before. That said, any issues that may pop up as a result of alcohol or opportunity are flagged here for documentation and care in case that becomes of any relevance in ED or further steps down the road she needs.
